body_txt: In the ancient fable of Androcles and the Lion, the runaway slave Androcles pulls a thorn from a lion's paw. In return, the lion befriends him, and shares his food. Eventually he is recaptured and sentenced to death by wild animals. The most fearsome beast turns out to be that very same lion he long ago befriended, and it refuses to harm him. Impressed, the Emperor grants both Androcles and the lion their freedom. Just as in this fable, a character befriends or aids a wild creature . In a moment of need, the creature recognizes this human, and returns the favour. Sometimes the creature was a Pet Baby Wild Animal , and the character wonders " Why Isn't It Attacking? " before recognizing it. While a pessimist will claim that No Good Deed Goes Unpunished , this Trope can often prove the exact opposite, and sometimes save the hero from certain doom. Hitchhiker Heroes often acquire animals like this; they are often Talking Animals , perhaps even humans trapped in an animal's body . For your safety , however, please note that this is generally not Truth in Television , and the real life examples listed below are incredibly rare. The vast majority of animals are simply not intelligent enough to grasp the ethical concepts involved in this sort of thing . In fact, most won't even be able to realize that you're trying to help them, and will instead associate you with whatever situation they're in, and lash out at you. And even if you're dealing with one of the rare handful of animals that are intelligent enough to both understand that you helped them and be thankful for it, those animals are by that very fact also intelligent enough for different individuals to have different personalities, so even then there's a chance that the particular individual you saved happens to be too much of a Jerkass to care. Unless you are a trained professional , DO. NOT. APPROACH. WILD. ANIMALS. THAT. ARE. IN. DISTRESS. Instead, contact a wildlife rescue immediately, there's no risk involved with a call. See Character Witness when this Trope applies to humans and other sentient creatures. A specific kind of Chekhov's Gunman . See also: Predator Turned Protector , Interspecies Friendship , I Owe You My Life , Disproportionate Reward , Character Witness , Rampage from a Nail , Karmic Jackpot , Because You Were Nice to Me , and Come Back, My Pet! . Contrast with The Farmer and the Viper , where the saved creature is nowhere near as grateful. Also contrast Rudeness Backfire , for when being rude comes back to bite someone. If the animal in question is some form of mythological creature, then see Supernaturally-Aided Escape .
